coredata.dc:title: CQDs modified TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x heterojunction photoanode with abundant oxygen vacancies for enhanced photoelectrochemical water oxidation

Zhang, Yingjie

coredata.dc:description: An efficient CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x heterojunction photoanode was designed and fabricated to enhance the photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance of hematite for water oxidation. The key innovation of this work lies in the construction of an oxygen-vacancy-rich TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x heterojunction photoanode by using Ti3C2 MXene as the precursor of TiO2 through a one-pot calcination process. The two main advantages of the present synthetic strategy are introducing abundant oxygen vacancies into the photoanode and preserving the nanostructure of the photoanode at high temperatures. During the synthesis, Ti3C2 MXene was oxidized to form TiO2, while Sn-FeOOH was simultaneously converted into Sn-Fe2O3-x nanorods. The high reducibility of Ti3C2 MXene and the doping of Sn4 + played a critical role in introducing abundant oxygen vacancies into Sn-Fe2O3-x. The concurrent formation of TiO2 and Sn-Fe2O3-x effectively prevented the aggregation of α-Fe2O3 nanorods at the high calcination temperature of 800°C and promoted close contact between the TiO2 and Sn-Fe2O3-x, reducing the interfacial transfer resistance. Lastly, the incorporation of CQDs further increased the light absorption ability and PEC performance of the heterojunction photoanode. The CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode achieved a photocurrent density of 1.47 mA/cm² at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which was 3.34 times higher than the α-Fe2O3. The enhanced PEC performance can be attributed to the introduction of abundant oxygen vacancies and the maintenance of its nanostructure at high temperatures, which can improve the donor density, charge transfer efficiency and light harvesting ability of the resultant photoanode.

head.ce:title: CQDs modified TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x heterojunction photoanode with abundant oxygen vacancies for enhanced photoelectrochemical water oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering, Xiangtan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Na Dai and Yingjie Zhang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x heterojunction photoanode was designed and fabricated to enhance the photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance of hematite for water oxidation. The key innovation of this work lies in the construction of an oxygen-vacancy-rich TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x heterojunction photoanode by using Ti3C2 MXene as the precursor of TiO2 through a one-pot calcination process. The two main advantages of the present synthetic strategy are introducing abundant oxygen vacancies into the photoanode and preserving the nanostructure of the photoanode at high temperatures. During the synthesis, Ti3C2 MXene was oxidized to form TiO2, while Sn-FeOOH was simultaneously converted into Sn-Fe2O3-x nanorods. The high reducibility of Ti3C2 MXene and the doping of Sn4 + played a critical role in introducing abundant oxygen vacancies into Sn-Fe2O3-x. The concurrent formation of TiO2 and Sn-Fe2O3-x effectively prevented the aggregation of α-Fe2O3 nanorods at the high calcination temperature of 800°C and promoted close contact between the TiO2 and Sn-Fe2O3-x, reducing the interfacial transfer resistance. Lastly, the incorporation of CQDs further increased the light absorption ability and PEC performance of the heterojunction photoanode. The CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode achieved a photocurrent density of 1.47 mA/cm² at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which was 3.34 times higher than the α-Fe2O3. The enhanced PEC performance can be attributed to the introduction of abundant oxygen vacancies and the maintenance of its nanostructure at high temperatures, which can improve the donor density, charge transfer efficiency and light harvesting ability of the resultant photoanode.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Along with the development of modern industries, the rapid growth of fossil fuel consumption has caused serious energy crisis and environmental pollution. The search for clean energy is crucial to maintaining a clean and livable environment in modern society. Hydrogen is an ideal clean energy due to its carbon free and high energy density characteristics [1–3]. Among the current advanced technologies to produce hydrogen, photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting is promising because the sunlight is utilized to trigger the reaction, which is recognized as a renewable and abundant energy source. The materials to construct photoelectrodes (photoanodes or photocathodes) are usually based on transition metal oxides, nitrides or sulfides [4–6]. Hematite (α-Fe2O3) is deemed as one of the most promising photoanode candidates due to its excellent visible light absorption (bandgap of 1.9–2.1 eV), high physical and chemical stability, environmental friendliness, and low cost. However, due to its improper edge position, low conductivity, short hole diffusion length (2–4 nm), and sluggish oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics, the PEC performance of the α-Fe2O3 photoanode is still quite insufficient [7,8].

Various strategies have been considered to address these limitations of the α-Fe2O3 photoanode. Doping metal or non-metal elements [9,10], modifications of oxygen evolution cocatalysts [11], constructions of heterojunctions [12], fabrications of nanostructures [13,14], etc. are effective to enhance the light harvesting ability, promote the charge transfer, facilitate the OER kinetics and thus improve the PEC performance of hematite. Arzaee et al. [15] successfully converted hematite (α-Fe2O3) nanoflakes into nanoflowers using aerosol-assisted chemical vapor deposition (AACVD) by supplying large amount of oxygen and optimizing the deposition time. The obtained α-Fe2O3 nanoflowers exhibited higher catalytic activity because of their large surface area and good light absorption. Wang et al. [16] prepared dense α-Fe2O3 nanosheet arrays by simple thermal oxidation treatment. This one-dimensional α-Fe2O3 nanostructure was more favorable for the transport of the photogenerated carriers and thus enhanced the PEC performance. Noted that the synthesis of these nanostructures strongly depends on the preparation strategies. However, the obtained nanostructures are usually prone to agglomeration at high temperatures. It is hard to maintain the nanostructures of these α-Fe2O3 if further modifications on these α-Fe2O3 require high temperatures, which are usually essential to construct efficient hematite photoanodes. Therefore, exploring facile synthetic strategies to maintain the morphology of nanostructural α-Fe2O3 at high temperatures is quite meaningful.

Introducing Fe2+ (e.g. oxygen vacancies) into the α-Fe2O3 photoanode is another effective way to enhance its PEC performance, which can increase the donor density of hematite electrodes owing to the introduction of Fe2+ and improve the charge transfer efficiency of hematite electrodes [17–19]. However, it should be noted that the introduction of Fe2+ or oxygen vacancies is often accompanied with low-temperature or anoxic conditions of preparation. A high temperature or sufficient oxygen environment would favor the formation of well-crystalline α-Fe2O3 [20]. The rigorous synthetic conditions to introduce Fe2+ or oxygen vacancies would confine the further modifications on these defected α-Fe2O3. Therefore, it is quite necessary to develop new synthetic strategies to flexibly introduce oxygen vacancies into α-Fe2O3 under more variable conditions, such as in air or at high temperatures. At high temperatures, Sn4+ can be incorporated into the Fe3+ sites of the hematite lattice through a substitutional mechanism [21]. During this process, Sn4+ is reduced to Sn2+, and the missing electrons in the system are compensated by the removal of oxygen ions (O2−). Consequently, excess electrons accumulate around the Fe3+ sites, leading to the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+. This also implies the consumption of some oxygen ions in the lattice, resulting in the formation of oxygen vacancies [22]. Based on the analysis, we propose introducing Sn4+ into FeOOH, the precursor of α-Fe2O3, via a simple impregnation method. After subsequent one-pot calcination, Fe2+ and oxygen vacancies can be introduced into α-Fe2O3 through redox reactions.

Constructing heterojunctions is a broadly adopted and effective strategy to improve the PEC properties of photoelectrodes. As for the α-Fe2O3 photoanode, the formation of heterojunctions and the synergistic effects induced through the interactions between the components yield a wide range of advantages, such as enhanced light harvesting, faster carrier transport and separation, and extended lifetime of electron-hole pairs. TiO2 is often used to construct heterojunctions with α-Fe2O3. Deng et al. [23] designed and prepared advanced TiO2/Fe2TiO5/Fe2O3 three-phase heterojunctions through a facile ion-exchange combined with calcination (IEC) method. Wang et al. [24] prepared TiO2/α-Fe2O3 heterojunction by hydrothermal method. Anita et al. [25] proposed an extended type I heterojunction between anatase TiO2 nanocrystals and α-Fe2O3 hematite nanocrystals. However, the most common strategy for a better contact between the two phases, α-Fe2O3 and TiO2, is to increase the calcination temperature. As mentioned above, the high temperature condition is usually unfavorable to the nanostructures of α-Fe2O3 and the introduction of Fe2+ into α-Fe2O3. Therefore, exploring a synthetic strategy which can maintain the nanostructures of α-Fe2O3 without agglomeration and simultaneously introduce Fe2+ into the α-Fe2O3 at high temperatures and air environment is of great significance to construct efficient α-Fe2O3 based heterojunctions.

Ti3C2 MXene, as a member of the 2D carbide (MXenes) family, possesses unique 2D morphology, excellent metallic conductivity, tunable surface chemistry, and outstanding electrochemical stability. These advantages qualify it an ideal candidate material for supercapacitors, battery materials and electrocatalysts [26–28]. Additionally, due to its stable physical properties, Ti3C2 MXene can serve as a robust support material when converted into TiO2 or other semiconductors, improving the stability and catalytic efficiency of the resultant heterojunctions [29]. Therefore, we propose Ti3C2 MXene as the in-situ source of Ti, providing nucleation sites for the growth of TiO2, which would form a stable TiO2 phase and create a close contact heterojunction with Sn-Fe2O3-x.

On the above basis, Ti3C2 MXene was adopted as the precursor of TiO2, and a CQDs modified TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x heterojunction photoanode (CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x) with a nanorod structure was successfully prepared. Along with the oxidation of Ti3C2 MXene to TiO2, the nanorod structure of α-Fe2O3 was maintained at a high temperature of 800 ℃. Abundant oxygen vacancies were introduced due to the reducibility of Ti3C2 MXene and Sn4+ doping. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were lastly modified on the heterojunction to obtain the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode, which exhibited superior PEC performance to the α-Fe2O3 for water oxidation.

Chemical reagents, Ti3AlC2 (Forsman, 98 %), Hydrofluoric acid (HF, 40 %, AR, Macklin), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, AR, Macklin), Iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 99 %, Sinopharm), Fluorine-doped SnO2 (FTO, 2.2 mm, 1 cm × 2 cm, transmittance>90 %, resistance <14 Ω/cm2, Nippon Sheet Glass, Japan), Urea (CH4N2O, Macklin), Tin tetrachloride (SnCl4·5H2O, ≥99.7 %, Aladdin), Acetone (CH3COCH3, ≥99.5 %, Sinopharm), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥97 %, Macklin), Anhydrous glucose (C6H12O6, 99 %, Maclean), Anhydrous sodium sulfite anhydrous (Na2SO3, ≥98 %, Macklin), Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %, Aladdin). Deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ cm of resistivity) was purified using a ZOOMWO-M purification system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ultra-thin Ti3C2 MXene nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: 1 g of Ti3AlC2 was slowly added to 12 mL of HF solution and stirred at room temperature (R.T.) for 24 hours. The etched suspension was then washed with DI water and centrifuged until pH≥ 6. The resulting powder was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 ℃ overnight. Slowly add 1 g of dried Ti3C2 to 15 mL DMSO and stir for 24 h. The mixture was then washed with DI water and centrifuged 3–5 times to remove residual DMSO in the mixture. Disperse the washed sample into DI water. The ratio of sample to DI water is approximately 1:500. Ultrasonic peeling was performed under Ar atmosphere. After ultrasonic for 5 h, the obtained mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min and the supernatant was taken to remove unpeeled Ti3C2 MXene. The supernatant was then suction filtered and vacuum dried at room temperature overnight to obtain ultra-thin Ti3C2 MXene nanosheets.

Weigh a certain amount of ultra-thin Ti3C2 MXene powder and disperse it in absolute ethanol. The solution was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 45 minutes to ensure an even dispersion of Ti3C2 MXene, and then reserved for later use. A clean FTO substrate was then placed in a spin coating machine. The ethanol dispersion of Ti3C2 MXene was spin-coated at 2000 rpm for 20 seconds on the FTO substrate. The FTO substrate was then transferred to a tube furnace and calcined at 350°C for 30 minutes. The temperature was increased at a rate of 10 °C/min. Precisely weigh 0.405 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 0.18 g of urea, stir magnetically for 20 minutes in 20 mL of DI water to make FeCl3 solution, and then transfer the solution to a 25 mL of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) lined reactor. Prior to transferring the FeCl3 solution, the FTO substrate (1 cm*2 cm*0.2 cm) was carefully placed in the PTFE reactor with a certain angle to ensure optimal exposure. Then seal the PTFE liner with a stainless steel autoclave and place it in an oven at 100℃ for 5 hours (hydrothermal reaction). After the reaction was completed, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature. The FTO glass was then removed, rinsed with DI water, and dried. Then the FTO glass was immersed in an alcholic solution of SnCl4·5H2O (0.1 M) for 5–15 minutes. The dried sample was put in a tube furnace and calcined in air at 800℃ for 10 minutes to obtain the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode.

CQDs were prepared based on previous reports [30,31]. Precisely weigh and add 2 g of NaOH to 100 mL of DI water, stir well, and then add 9 g of glucose to the solution. The solution was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 2 hours, and then hydrochloric acid (3 M) was added to adjust the pH to neutral to obtain a CQDs solution. The TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode was immersed in the CQDs solution for 9 hours, removed and dried overnight at room temperature. The CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode was obtained. Fig. 1 showed the synthetic route of the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterizations of the fabricated photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: The phase composition and crystal structure of different photoanodes were studied using X-ray diffraction (XRD) as shown in Fig. 2a. Referring to the standard XRD pattern of α-Fe2O3 (PDF # 33–0664), the characteristic peaks of α-Fe2O3 and FTO in Fig. 2a indicates that the α-Fe2O3 has been successfully grown on the FTO substrate. The diffraction peaks at 33.1°, 35.6° and 64° belong to the (104), (110) and (300) planes of α-Fe2O3 [32], respectively. As for the XRD pattern of Sn-Fe2O3-x, the characteristic peak around 38° (SnO2) is greatly enhanced, suggesting that tin doping of α-Fe2O3 using the impregnation method improves the crystallinity of SnO2. In addition, the XRD pattern of TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x shows that the peak at 35.6° greatly increases compared with that of Sn-Fe2O3-x. Due to the TiO2 modification, the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x heterojunction shows (110) orientation preference of α-Fe2O3, which boosts the peak at 35.6° and is very important for the improved conductivity of the photoanode [33]. The XRD pattern of TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x does not contain the characteristic peaks of TiO2, which may be due to the small loading amount of TiO2 that is out of the detecting limit of XRD. In short, the XRD patterns of the α-Fe2O3, Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanodes do not present obvious differences, suggesting that Sn4+ doping, forming heterojunction with TiO2 and CQDs modification would not influence the crystal structure of α-Fe2O3 significantly.

The light absorption capacity of semiconductors significantly influences their PEC performance. UV visible diffuse reflection measurements were performed to study the light absorption of obtained samples. Fig. 2b shows the UV visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) of different photoanodes. The absorption curves of the α-Fe2O3, Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanodes are similar, with absorption band edges at around 600 nm. As shown in Fig. S1, the bandgap widths of the α-Fe2O3, Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x can be estimated as 2.01, 1.99 and 1.98 eV, respectively, based on the Tauc plot: (αhν) 1/2=C(hν-Eg) [34]. Therefore, Sn4+ doping and TiO2 modification have limited influence on the bandgap of α-Fe2O3, which is similar to the previous report [35]. The great enhancement in absorbance of the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x from 300 to 700 nm in Fig. 2b is owing to the fact that CQDs can act as photosensitizers and increase absorption across the entire light spectrum [36]. The bandgap width of CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x is narrowed to about 1.96 eV which extends the light absorption capacity of the α-Fe2O3 (2.01 eV).

FT-IR is broadly used to examine the presence of CQDs as well as their surface structure, functional groups and linkages [37]. Fig. 2c shows the FT-IR spectra of the α-Fe2O3, Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x. Compared with the α-Fe2O3, Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x, the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x contains an absorption at 3270 cm−1, representing the O-H stretching vibration [38]. The absorption near 1033 cm−1 is attributed to the asymmetric vibration of the C-O group [33,34] and the absorption near 500 cm−1 is the C-H bend [39,40]. These results suggest the existence of O-H groups on the surface of CQDs. Based on the FT-IR spectra, the CQDs have been successfully impregnated on the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x. Due to the O-H, C-O and C-H groups on its surface [41], the CQDs are highly hydrophilic and favorable to the contact between the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode and water, which is believed to possess unparalleled advantages in PEC water splitting processes [42].

Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was employed to investigate the oxidation states and chemical environments of the fabricated photoanodes. As shown in Fig. 2d, there is a strong EPR signal with the g value around 2.003, which is consistent with the g value of oxygen vacancies (2.000) [43]. The EPR results indicate the presence of oxygen vacancies in the α-Fe2O3, Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanodes. Interestingly, the intensity of the EPR signal is slightly enhanced with tin doping, and further enhanced greatly after forming the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x heterojunction. It is reported that Sn4+ doping can form the oxygen vacancies in α-Fe2O3 due to the charge balance [21] which would be further verified by the XPS results as discussed later. The formation of heterojunction with TiO2 produces even more oxygen vacancies because the Ti3C2 MXene (due to its native reducibility) can partially reduce the Fe3+ to Fe2+ in α-Fe2O3 when it is oxidized and converted to TiO2. The intensity of the EPR signal is further enhanced with the modification of CQDs, indicating the abundant oxygen vacancies in the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x. The oxygen vacancies are believed to promote the interfacial OER kinetics [44]. Therefore, the current synthesis presents a novel strategy to fabricate oxygen vacancies enriched α-Fe2O3 photoanode.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was adopted to characterize the chemical elements and chemical valence states in the photoanodes. As shown in Fig. 5f, the main elements in the α-Fe2O3, Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x are composed of Fe, O, Sn, and C. Ti element can be detected in the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x. Fig. 5e shows the Sn 3d spectra of the photoanodes. The binding energies of Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2 in the α-Fe2O3 (on FTO glass) and Sn-Fe2O3-x are located at 494.8 and 486.4 eV, respectively. Notably, in the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x, the peaks of Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2 shift to the lower binding energies by 0.1 and 0.3 eV, respectively and in the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x, the peaks of Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2 shift to the lower binding energies by 0.1 and 0.2 eV, respectively. A lower binding energy is believed to favor the charge transfer on the surface of the photoanodes [45], which implies that the modifications of CQDs and TiO2 can favor the charge transfer. Fig. 5a shows the C 1s spectra and the peak at 284.8 eV for the three photoanodes is from the adventitious carbon. Compared with the α-Fe2O3 and Sn-Fe2O3-x, the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x contains an additional peak at 288.5 eV, representing the CO bond. The CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x shows two additional peaks at 288.2 and 286.5 eV, representing the OC-O and C-O bonds, respectively, which suggests the successful modification of CQDs [46]. The Fe 2p spectra of the photoanodes are shown in Fig. 5b. For the α-Fe2O3, the binding energies of Fe 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 are located at 724.5 and 711.6 eV, respectively, which are consistent with the previous report [47]. For the Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x, the binding energy of Fe 2p3/2 shifts to a lower binding energy of 711.2 eV by 0.4 eV. And for the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x, the binding energy of Fe 2p3/2 shifts to a lower binding energy of 710.8 eV by 0.8 eV. Notably, for the Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanodes, a shoulder peak of Fe2+ appears at a binding energy of 715.8 eV. By comparing the relative peak intensities of the characteristic binding energies of Fe3+ and Fe2+ in the Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanodes, it is worth noting that the amount of Fe2+ in the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x is larger than that in the Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x. Doping Sn4+ can induce the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ in the α-Fe2O3 for the sake of charge balance [21]. Moreover, in the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x, the amount of Fe2+ is greatly enhanced compared with that in the Sn-Fe2O3-x because the reducibility of Ti3C2 MXene leads to the partial reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ during the calcination of Sn-FeOOH. The XPS results of Fe 2p are perfectly in accordance with the EPR data, which also indicates the gradual increase of oxygen vacancies in the Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x. Fig. 5c shows the O 1 s spectra of the photoanodes. For α-Fe2O3, the OV peak at approximately 531.4 eV corresponds to oxygen vacancies [48], while the OL peak at around 529.8 eV is attributed to lattice oxygen [48]. After the introduction of CQDs, a new OC peak appears at approximately 532.6 eV, which is assigned to chemisorbed OH- [49]. Based on the fitted peak areas, the relative proportions of OV in the photoanodes were further calculated. The α-Fe2O3-x, Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x, and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanodes contain relative proportions of 28 %, 42 %, 44 %, and 48 % of OV, respectively as shown in Table S1. It is evident that the intensity of the OV peak is significantly enhanced after doping with Sn4+ and the introduction of TiO2, indicating the successful incorporation of oxygen vacancies. In the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode, the OC peak can be attributed to the hydrophilicity of CQDs, which promotes the formation of surface hydroxyl groups and chemisorbed oxygen, thereby enhancing the PEC performance. Fig. 5d is the Ti 2p spectra of the photoanodes. The binding energies at 458.2 and 464.0 eV of TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x (458.3 and 464.1 eV of CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x) correspond to the Ti4+, which implies that Ti3C2 MXene is completely transformed to TiO2 during hydrothermal and calcination processes. The XPS results further certify the introduction of Fe2+ and the successful fabrication of CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x. The EPR and XPS results mutually support the presence and enhancement of oxygen vacancies in the photoanodes. The EPR results provide direct evidence for the existence of oxygen vacancies, which is enhanced with Sn-doping and the formation of the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x heterojunction. This is further corroborated by the XPS data, where shifts in the binding energies of Fe 2p and the increased intensity of the O 1 s peak suggest the formation of oxygen vacancies, particularly in the modified samples. The combination of both characterizations provides a comprehensive understanding of the oxygen vacancies' contribution to the enhanced charge transfer and interfacial kinetics. Together, with EPR indicating their existence and XPS revealing their impact on the material's electronic structure, these characterizations reinforce the role of oxygen vacancies in improving the PEC performance.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was adopted to study the microstructure changes of the fabricated photoanodes. As shown in Fig. 3a, the α-Fe2O3 nanorods grow vertically and separately along the FTO substrate. After Sn4+ doping via calcination at 800℃, the separate α-Fe2O3 nanorods merge together and form clusters in Fig. 3b. The SEM images of TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x are shown in Fig. 3c. Compared with the α-Fe2O3 and Sn-Fe2O3-x, the growth direction of TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x nanorods is not vertical. It is because the pre-spin coated Ti3C2 MXene is oxidized to TiO2, which is adhered to the FTO substrate and influences the surface flatness of the FTO substrate. Interestingly and importantly, although prepared through calcination at 800℃, the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x nanorods (Fig. 3c) are still evenly separated without any aggregation in contrast to the Sn-Fe2O3-x (Fig. 3b). By comparing Fig. 3c with Fig. 3a, the surface of the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x nanorods becomes rough and the diameter of the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x nanorods is even a little smaller than that of the α-Fe2O3 nanorods. Noted that in the present synthetic strategy, the oxidation of Ti3C2 MXene to TiO2 takes place along with the reduction of Sn-FeOOH to Sn-Fe2O3-x simultaneously in a one-pot calcination. For the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x, the TiO2 derived from Ti3C2 MXene may prevent the α-Fe2O3 nanorods from aggregation at 800℃. In addition, the α-Fe2O3 would be partially reduced to α-Fe2O3-x by the Ti3C2 MXene during the calcination, which may be the reason that the TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x nanorods possess a rougher surface and smaller diameter than the α-Fe2O3 nanorods. There is no obvious difference in Fig. 3c and d, suggesting that the modification of CQDs would not change the microstructure of the photoanodes. The SEM results suggest that the current synthetic strategy is effective to prevent the α-Fe2O3 nanorods from aggregation at a very high temperature and achieve thin α-Fe2O3 nanorods, which is usually considered as a challenge in the α-Fe2O3 synthesis. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was used to observe the lattice patterns of the samples in Fig. 4. The lattice fringes of α-Fe2O3, TiO2, and CQDs can be measured, and the dimensions, shape, and dispersion of the CQDs are further revealed in Fig. 4c. The CQDs exhibit a nearly spherical morphology with an average size of approximately 5 nm and show good dispersion on the material surface. These observations confirm the successful decorations of TiO2 and CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectrochemical measurements of the fabricated photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions for the PEC measurements of the α-Fe2O3, Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanodes are included in the Supplementary Material. In order to investigate the effects of Sn4+ doping, TiO2 and CQDs modifications on α-Fe2O3, the impregnation time of SnCl4 (5, 10, or 15 minutes), the concentration of Ti3C2 MXene suspension for spin coating (0.5, 1, or 2 mg/mL), and the impregnation time of CQDs solution (6, 9, or 12 hours) were optimized and shown in Fig. S2. When the immersion time of SnCl4 was 10 minutes, the concentration of Ti3C2 MXene suspension was 1 mg /mL, and the immersion time of CQDs solution was 9 hours, the Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x, and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanodes achieved the highest photocurrent densities.

The linear scan voltammetry (LSV) curves of the resultant photoanodes were measured with a three-electrode system in 1 M NaOH electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 6a, at 1.23 V (vs. RHE), the photocurrent densities of the α-Fe2O3, Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanodes are 0.44, 0.63, 0.98 and 1.47 mA cm−2, respectively. The photocurrent density of CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x is 3.34 times higher than that of α-Fe2O3. The photocurrent density of the obtained CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode is higher or comparable to that of α-Fe2O3 based photoanodes in recent reports as listed in Table S2. The onset potential of pristine α-Fe2O3 NRs is found to be about 0.80 V (vs. RHE) in Fig. 6a. However, Sn4+ doping results in an anodic shift (about 100 mV) of the onset potential for the photoanodes of Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x. This implies that while Sn4+ doping can enhance the photocurrent density of the α-Fe2O3 photoanode, it would limit its surface kinetics of OER. Similar anodic shifts (from 100 to 200 mV) of the onset potential for tin-doped hematite have been reported previously [50,51], suggesting that Sn4+ doping can lead to slow surface kinetics of OER due to the introduction of surface states [52]. Interestingly, the onset potential of CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x presents a significant cathodic shift (about 0.67 V vs. RHE) compared with that of α-Fe2O3, which indicates that the modification of CQDs can effectively favor the OER kinetics of α-Fe2O3. It can be attributed to the decent conductivity and hydrophilicity of CQDs [42,53]. The transient photocurrent density versus time (I-T) curves with chopped light of different photoanodes at 1.23 V (vs. RHE) are displayed in Fig. 6b. The photocurrent promptly appears and vanishes with the illumination on and off, revealing that the obtained photoanodes possess a rapid photoresponse. The I-T plots also indicate that during the measurement duration of 420 seconds, there is no significant decrease in all photoanodes. The PEC stability of the resultant photoanodes is further evaluated by recording the J-t curve for 1800 s in Fig. S3d. The photocurrent density of all photoanodes exhibits negligible decrease, revealing the high PEC stability of the photoanodes. Additionally, the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode after stability test was further characterized by XRD, SEM, and XPS, and the results were shown in Fig. S6. After the stability test, the characteristic XRD peaks and the nanorod structure of α-Fe2O3 were preserved, indicating the stability of the crystal structure and microstructure of the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode. XPS analysis revealed the emergence of two new smaller Sn2+ peaks in the post-test sample, which was attributed to the generation of photo-induced electrons on the TiO2 surface during the test, causing partial electron migration to Sn4+ and leading to the conversion of a small amount of Sn4+ to Sn2+ [54]. It may be the reason that the photocurrent density of the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode exhibits a slight decrease during the stability test, which needs to be solved in our future study.

In order to study the incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of different photoanodes, the photocurrent density in the wavelength range of 350–700 nm was measured at 1.23 V (vs. RHE) as shown in Fig. 7a. All photoanodes exhibit PEC activity mainly at 350–600 nm. The IPCE of CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x at 350 nm is 33.0 %, which is much higher than that of α-Fe2O3 (13.8 %), Sn-Fe2O3-x (22.4 %) and TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x (25.7 %). The applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) of different photoanodes is presented in Fig. 7b. The maximum ABPE value of α-Fe2O3 is 0.036 % at 1.04 V (vs. RHE). After Sn4+ doping and TiO2 modification, the ABPE values of Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x increase to 0.037 % and 0.062 %, respectively. The maximum ABPE value of CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x is up to 0.190 % at a bias of 0.98 V (vs. RHE). The CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode exhibits the highest ABPE value, which is 5.28 times higher than that of α-Fe2O3. Therefore, by doping Sn4+, coupling with TiO2 and modifying CQDs, the photon utilization efficiency of the α-Fe2O3 photoanode can be improved significantly.

To further investigate the charge transport of the fabricated photoanodes, AC impedance measurements under AM 1.5 G illumination were carried out at 1.23 V (vs. RHE). The fitting results and an equivalent circuit model are shown in Fig. 7c. The circle radius of the Nyquist curve in EIS is proportional to the charge transfer resistance at the semiconductor or electrolyte interface. The CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x has the lowest impedance compared with the other photoanodes, indicating that the interface transfer barrier is the lowest. For quantitative analysis, the impedance values are calculated by simulation software, and the results are presented in Table S3. The fitting values of Rct2 for the α-Fe2O3, Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanodes are 88207, 37049, 15264 and 3618 Ω, respectively, which decease gradually and greatly. It indicates that the present strategy can effectively lower the charge transfer resistance of α-Fe2O3 and the surface charge of CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x is much easier to transfer than that of α-Fe2O3. The Mott Schottky (M-S) measurements are shown in Fig. 7d. Since α-Fe2O3 is an n-type semiconductor with photogenerated electrons as the main carriers, the slopes of the tangents of M-S curves are positive for all the photoanodes. In the M-S plots, the carrier concentration of the photoanode is inversely proportional to the slope of the tangent line. From Fig. 7d, it can be seen that the slopes of the tangent lines decrease sequentially after Sn4+ doping, TiO2 coupling and CQDs modification, implying that the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode has the highest carrier density. The high carrier density can favor the charge transport and inhibit the recombination of photoinduced carriers of the fabricated photoanode.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy is further used to analyze the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs because the charge recombination in semiconductors can lead to photoluminescence [55]. The recombination level of electron-hole pairs can be inferred from the intensity of their PL signals. The weaker the PL signal appears, the higher the separation efficiency will be. Fig. S3 shows the PL spectra of the α-Fe2O3, Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x, and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanodes. Under excitation at a wavelength of 350 nm, all samples display PL peaks with similar shapes. Additionally, a slight shift towards lower wavelengths is observed in the maximum absorption position of CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x compared to the other samples, which is attributed to the modification of CQDs affecting the recombination time difference of the excited states and the carrier transfer effects between the two states [56]. Compared with the other samples, the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x shows the weakest PL signal, indicating that TiO2 and CQDs modifications can effectively suppress the recombination of photogenerated carriers. By adding the hole scavenger of Na2SO3, the LSV curves of resultant photoanodes were measured and shown in Fig. S4. The charge injection (ηinj) and separation (ηsep) efficiencies of obtained photoanodes were calculated and depicted in Fig. 7e and f. At 1.23 V (vs. RHE), the charge injection efficiencies (ηinj) of α-Fe2O3, Sn-Fe2O3-x, TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x and CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x are 59.5 %, 66.3 %, 81.7 % and 87.2 %, respectively. The charge separation (ηsep) efficiency of CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x (10.7 %) is also higher than that of α-Fe2O3 (8.8 %), Sn-Fe2O3-x (9.4 %), and TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x (9.7 %) at 1.23 V (vs. RHE). Interestingly, the ηinj of Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x are lower than that of α-Fe2O3 at the low applied bias region as shown in Fig. 7e. It is because Sn4+ doping can result in an anodic shift of the onset potential for the photoanodes of Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x as discussed previously in Fig. 6a. The modification of CQDs can remarkably boost the ηinj across the measuring applied bias which is attributed to the decent conductivity and hydrophilicity of CQDs that can effectively favor the surface OER kinetics of α-Fe2O3. As shown in Fig. 7f, the ηsep is also increased from 8.8 % (α-Fe2O3) to 10.7 % (CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x), suggesting the enhanced charge separation efficiency in the bulk of the composite photoanode.

The open circuit potential (OCP) values for all photoanodes were obtained by evaluating the differences between the OCP values measured under “dark” and “light” conditions. As shown in Fig. 8, Sn4+ doping significantly increased the OCP value of α-Fe2O3 from 0.05 V to 0.12 V. After the formation of heterojunction with TiO2, the OCP value further increased to 0.17 V. After modifying CQDs, the OCP value reached the highest value of 0.20 V. These results are consistent with the LSV, the I-T, and EIS findings. A higher OCP corresponds to a higher photovoltage at the electrode surface [57], indicating a stronger driving force for PEC water oxidation. By introducing oxygen vacancies, constructing heterojunctions, and modifying with CQDs, the charge separation efficiency and charge transport dynamics of the obtained photoanode were improved, which contributed to the enhanced OCP.

As shown in Fig. 9, the UPS and Tauc analyses were performed on the individual components of Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2 synthesized under the same experimental conditions in order to accurately analyze the band structures of the samples. The UPS spectra for the single components of Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2 are shown in Fig. 9a and c, from which the valence bands (VB) of Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2 are determined to be 4.02 eV and 3.46 eV, respectively. The corresponding Tauc analyses are presented in Fig. 9b and d, where the band gap widths for the Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2 are calculated to be 1.99 eV and 3.19 eV, respectively. The band gap width represents the energy difference between the conduction band (CB) and the VB, allowing the CBs of Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2 to be determined as 6.02 eV and 6.65 eV, respectively. The detailed energy level distribution is shown in Fig. S5. Based on the energy band alignment, it can be concluded that the constitutive heterojunction type of Sn-Fe2O3-x and TiO2 is a type I heterojunction.

Based on the above discussions, a probable mechanism of the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode for the enhanced PEC water oxidation is proposed in Fig. 10. The HOMO and LUMO potential levels of the CQDs can be found in literatures [58]. Based on the appropriate potential positions of the VBs and HOMO, the photoinduced holes would transfer from the TiO2 to the Sn-Fe2O3-x, and then to the CQDs on the surface, which is a thermodynamically favorable process. The current design can effectively improve the sluggish OER kinetics of α-Fe2O3, thus boosting its PEC performance. With the applied bias, the photogenerated electrons would transfer from the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode to the Pt electrode and reduce H+ to H2. The narrow energy gap from the π orbital to the π* orbital in the CQDs [58] can also favor the light absorption of the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode.

In this work, an efficient CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x heterojunction photoanode with a nanorod structure was successfully fabricated and used for PEC water oxidation. We innovatively adopted Ti3C2 MXene as the TiO2 precursor. The CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode achieved a photocurrent density of 1.47 mA/cm2 at 1.23 vs. RHE, which was 3.34 times higher than the α-Fe2O3 photoanode. Notably, the Ti3C2 MXene was oxidized to TiO2 and the Sn-FeOOH was reduced to Sn-Fe2O3-x synchronously during the one-pot calcination process. The present synthetic strategy can effectively inhibit the agglomeration of α-Fe2O3 nanorods and maintain its nanorod structure at 800℃. In addition, the reducibility of Ti3C2 MXene and Sn4+ doping can introduce abundant oxygen vacancies into the heterojunction photoanode, which would enhance the donor density. The synchronous formations of the TiO2 and Sn-Fe2O3-x can also favor the achievement of the close contact heterojunction of TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x, which decreases the interfacial transfer resistance and facilitates the charge transport. Lastly, the CQDs decoration greatly improves the light absorption and the surface OER kinetics of the CQDs/TiO2/Sn-Fe2O3-x photoanode. By adopting Ti3C2 MXene as the TiO2 precursor, this work provides a novel strategy to maintain the microstructure of α-Fe2O3 at high temperatures, introduce abundant oxygen vacancies into α-Fe2O3 and fabricate efficient α-Fe2O3 based photoanodes.
